cognitive_warfare.significance: Structural — represents the AI capability transition from augmentation to primary production pipeline
cognitive_warfare.headline: AI-generated synthetic media as scalable FIMI infrastructure
cognitive_warfare.detail: DFRLab March 2026 documentation of 26-channel YouTube network (1.8B views) using AI synthetic anchors, LLM scripts, automated thumbnails represents operational proof-of-concept for AI-at-scale FIMI. Production cost per video negligible; reach algorithmic. This is the maturation of the AI-FIMI convergence flagged in Issue 1 (47% of EEAS-tracked operations AI-assisted). The YouTube model differs from text-seeding operations: video with synthetic anchors creates higher epistemic authority signals for audiences.
cognitive_warfare.summary: Structural — represents the AI capability transition from augmentation to primary production pipeline

cognitive_warfare.significance: Tactical — election-specific, but the meta-FIMI pattern is structurally persistent across all documented Russian electoral operations
cognitive_warfare.headline: Meta-FIMI: counter-narratives targeting interference reporting
cognitive_warfare.detail: Hungarian Conservative's March 23 article systematically contests the evidence base for Russian interference reporting ahead of the April 12 election. Consistent with known Russian active measures doctrine: seed doubt about the interference story itself, not just the primary narratives. This meta-layer — discrediting the attributors rather than the attributed operation — is MF1 territory and analytically distinct from legitimate methodological critique.
cognitive_warfare.summary: Tactical — election-specific, but the meta-FIMI pattern is structurally persistent across all documented Russian electoral operations

cognitive_warfare.significance: Doctrinal — EEAS shifting from reactive attribution to proactive pre-election deployment
cognitive_warfare.headline: EEAS Hybrid Rapid Response Team — Armenia June 2026
cognitive_warfare.detail: EEAS HRVP Kallas confirmed at the 2026 Counter-FIMI Conference (17 March) that a Hybrid Rapid Response Team will deploy to Armenia ahead of June 2026 parliamentary elections. First HRRТ deployment to the South Caucasus. Armenia was identified in EEAS FIMI Report 4th as an expected primary target in the run-up to June elections. Signals a doctrinal shift from post-election attribution to pre-election deterrence.
cognitive_warfare.summary: Doctrinal — EEAS shifting from reactive attribution to proactive pre-election deployment

cognitive_warfare.headline: X/Twitter transparency gap persists as structural monitoring blind spot.
cognitive_warfare.detail: X/Twitter has maintained zero Coordinated Inauthentic Behavior (CIB) equivalent disclosures since Q3 2022. API restrictions limit third-party monitoring. This creates persistent blind spot for all tracked actors, particularly for campaigns leveraging X/Twitter as primary amplification platform. Meta CIB, Google TAG, and Microsoft MSTIC continue regular disclosures; X/Twitter absence represents significant monitoring asymmetry.

cognitive_warfare.headline: EEAS institutional monitoring gap: US and Israeli FIMI operations systematically under-documented.
cognitive_warfare.detail: EEAS FIMI framework is calibrated for Russian and Chinese operations. US and Israeli information operations in European information space lack equivalent institutional tracking at Tier 1-2 level. This creates structural attribution asymmetry: absence of institutional documentation does not indicate absence of activity, but rather absence of institutional monitoring capacity. Recommend explicit acknowledgment of this gap in all US/IL attribution assessments.

cognitive_warfare.headline: AI-FIMI detection methodology gap: synthetic content generation outpaces forensic attribution.
cognitive_warfare.detail: Current Tier 1-3 monitoring relies on platform disclosures, government attributions, and OSINT pattern analysis. AI-generated disinformation campaigns may evade detection without explicit platform forensics. Russian Doppelganger and Chinese influencer networks increasingly leverage synthetic content. This week's null signal may reflect undetected AI-FIMI activity rather than genuine operational lull. Recommend development of AI-FIMI specific detection methodologies.

flags.headline: FIMI as constitutive mechanism of democratic erosion — Hungary election FIMI density at critical level
flags.linkage: Three simultaneous confirmed/assessed RU operations targeting Hungary April 12 election (SDA, Storm-1516, SVR Gamechanger) represent the highest documented pre-election FIMI density for a single EU election. The Democratic Integrity Monitor's Hungary election-adjacent entries should cross-reference this cluster. F1-flagged signal this week.

flags.headline: Cognitive warfare as operational preparation for European strategic paralysis — Hungary election as test case
flags.linkage: If Orbán retains power with confirmed Russian FIMI support, this consolidates a Russian-aligned veto actor inside the EU for the next cycle. The EGHTM tracks EU institutional coherence — this is the FIMI dimension of that question. Washington Post reporting on Szijjártó leaking EU meeting content to Lavrov (if confirmed) represents the intelligence capture dimension that EGHTM state-capture tracker should note.

flags.headline: AI-generated YouTube network (1.8B views) as operational proof of AI-FIMI convergence at scale
flags.linkage: DFRLab March 23 documentation of 26-channel AI production pipeline achieving 1.8B views is the most significant empirical data point on AI-FIMI convergence since the EEAS 47% figure. AI Governance Monitor should track platform policy gaps (YouTube not enforcing against synthetic anchors presenting fabricated events as news) as a governance failure with direct FIMI consequences.

flags.headline: Post-election FIMI lull following Slovenia parliamentary elections (March 22, 2026) may precede escalation cycle targeting upcoming EU member state elections. Recommend forward-looking monitoring for FIMI activity targeting 2026-2027 electoral cycles.

Iranian FIMI operations targeting Israel-Palestine narratives remain active at baseline. No escalation detected this week, but recommend continued monitoring for FIMI activity correlated with Middle East conflict escalation events.

Russian operations prioritise narrative persistence over operational security, accepting repeated platform takedowns as an operational cost in exchange for sustained narrative presence

Iranian operations have undergone a doctrinal shift from Persian-language diaspora targeting to English-language Western audience targeting, signalling intent to shape Western public opinion on Israel-Palestine narratives and US domestic politics
EEAS institutional perimeter excludes US and Israeli operations by political design, not evidentiary threshold, creating a structural asymmetry in FIMI monitoring that undermines field legitimacy

module_0.title: No new FIMI operations disclosed this week — platform reporting cadence gaps persist across all Tier 1 sources
module_0.body: The week ending 14 May 2026 produced zero new FIMI disclosures from Meta, Google TAG, Microsoft MSTIC, EEAS, Stanford IO, DFRLab, ASPI, or Bellingcat. Three ongoing campaigns (Doppelganger, Endless Mayfly, Portal Kombat) continue from established baselines with no new enforcement actions. The absence reflects structural disclosure gaps — Meta shifted to semiannual reporting, Google TAG Q1 2026 bulletin is overdue, and X/Twitter maintains no CIB-equivalent mechanism. Operational silence in the disclosure layer does not imply operational silence by threat actors.

underweighted.headline: Platform disclosure cadence gaps now exceed 10 weeks for Google TAG and 9 weeks for Meta CIB
underweighted.why_it_matters: Google TAG last published a bulletin on 29 January 2026 (Q4 2025). Meta last published an Adversarial Threat Report on 11 March 2026 (H1 2026). Both are overdue based on historical quarterly and semiannual cadences. The absence of new disclosures creates a structural blind spot in FIMI monitoring — threat actors continue to operate, but the primary institutional disclosure mechanisms are silent. This gap disproportionately affects attribution confidence for ongoing campaigns and prevents timely detection of new operations.
underweighted.body: Google TAG last published a bulletin on 29 January 2026 (Q4 2025). Meta last published an Adversarial Threat Report on 11 March 2026 (H1 2026). Both are overdue based on historical quarterly and semiannual cadences. The absence of new disclosures creates a structural blind spot in FIMI monitoring — threat actors continue to operate, but the primary institutional disclosure mechanisms are silent. This gap disproportionately affects attribution confidence for ongoing campaigns and prevents timely detection of new operations.
underweighted.asymmetric: The disclosure gap is not evenly distributed across actors. Russian and Chinese operations benefit most from the silence — both have documented high-volume infrastructure (Doppelganger, Spamouflage) that persists across platform takedowns. Iranian operations (IUVM) received updated attribution in March but no subsequent enforcement visibility. US and Israeli operations remain outside EEAS institutional perimeter by political design, compounding the asymmetry.

campaigns.platform: Multi-platform (X/Twitter, Telegram, TikTok, spoofed news domains)
campaigns.target: EU member states, Ukraine, US political audiences
campaigns.summary: Doppelganger is a persistent Russian influence operation first identified in 2022 that creates fake websites mimicking legitimate Western news outlets and amplifies pro-Russia, anti-Ukraine narratives. Stanford Internet Observatory confirmed that accounts linked to Doppelganger domains remained active on X/Twitter after Meta takedowns, posing as Western news outlets and posting in English and French. Some network nodes migrated to Telegram and TikTok following Meta enforcement actions. No new enforcement actions or disclosures specific to Doppelganger were published this week.
campaigns.asymmetric: Doppelganger demonstrates the limits of platform-level enforcement. Meta and Google TAG have conducted multiple takedowns since 2022, yet the operation persists via platform migration and domain regeneration. The operation signals a doctrine of narrative persistence — Russian actors prioritise continuity over stealth, accepting repeated exposure as an operational cost.

campaigns.summary: Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (published 11 March 2026) provided updated attribution for the persistent Iranian influence operation known as Endless Mayfly, linking it to Iran International Union of Virtual Media (IUVM). A separate Iranian operation began targeting English-speaking audiences in summer 2025 and was disrupted by Meta in Q4 2025 before it could build an authentic audience. Iran continues to rank as one of the three leading sources of foreign influence operations globally. No new disclosures this week.
campaigns.asymmetric: The IUVM attribution represents a rare instance of Meta naming a specific Iranian state-adjacent entity. This level of specificity is uncommon in Meta CIB reporting and signals high internal confidence. The expansion into English-language targeting marks a doctrinal shift — Iranian operations historically focused on Persian-language diaspora audiences.

campaigns.target: Western European audiences (EN, FR, DE, RU)
campaigns.summary: Google TAG Q1 2025 bulletin (last updated 15 May 2025) documented the blocking of 57 domains linked to the Russian actor publicly tracked as Portal Kombat, sharing content in English, French, German, Russian and other languages supportive of Russia. This operation targets Western European information environments with pro-Russia narratives. No new TAG bulletin covering Q1 2026 or Q2 2026 has been published as of the current collection window.
campaigns.asymmetric: Portal Kombat operates exclusively via YouTube domain blocking — no social media account networks have been publicly attributed to this actor. This suggests a content-distribution model rather than a social-engagement model. The operation prioritises reach over interaction, consistent with broadcast-style influence doctrine.

actors.posture: Multi-platform influence infrastructure combining fake news site spoofing (Doppelganger), IRA-linked YouTube channel networks, and AI-assisted content production. Operational doctrine emphasises narrative persistence across platform takedowns via migration to Telegram and TikTok.
actors.doctrine_note: Coordination between content production units and RT/Sputnik amplification personnel remains a documented feature. No new Russian FIMI disclosures this week; Doppelganger and Portal Kombat baselines persist from prior reporting.
actors.headline: No new Russian FIMI disclosures this week; Doppelganger and Portal Kombat baselines persist from prior reporting
actors.asymmetric: Russia remains the most prolific documented source of foreign influence operations across Tier 1 to 3 sources, with active infrastructure confirmed across YouTube, Telegram, X/Twitter, and spoofed news domains. The absence of new disclosures reflects reporting cadence gaps rather than operational cessation.

actors.posture: Primary documented influence infrastructure (Spamouflage/Taizi Flood) continues to operate at high volume, primarily via YouTube channel networks uploading spammy entertainment content with a small subset targeting US-China political narratives.
actors.doctrine_note: Operational doctrine emphasises volume and platform saturation over precision targeting. No new China-specific FIMI disclosures were published this week.
actors.headline: No new Chinese FIMI disclosures this week; Spamouflage/Taizi Flood baseline persists from prior TAG and Meta reporting
actors.asymmetric: China ranks as one of the three leading sources of foreign influence operations per Meta H1 2026 report. Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin documented ongoing large-scale YouTube channel terminations linked to PRC-affiliated operations. No new China-specific enforcement actions or attributions were published in the current collection window.

actors.posture: Influence operations (IUVM/Endless Mayfly, Cotton Sandstorm) increasingly target English-speaking Western audiences with AI-assisted content. Meta updated IUVM attribution and disrupted a new English-language operation in Q4 2025.
actors.doctrine_note: IRGC-linked operations maintain a focus on Israel-Palestine narratives and US domestic political targeting. Meta H1 2026 report updated IUVM attribution for Endless Mayfly, marking continued investment in Western-facing influence infrastructure.
actors.headline: Meta H1 2026 report updated IUVM attribution for Endless Mayfly; no new Iranian FIMI disclosures this week
actors.capability_change: Expansion into English-language targeting marks a doctrinal shift from historical Persian-language diaspora focus
actors.asymmetric: Iran continues to rank as one of the three leading sources of foreign influence operations globally per Meta. The IUVM attribution represents a rare instance of Meta naming a specific Iranian state-adjacent entity, signalling high internal confidence.

actors.posture: No documented FIMI operations in the current collection window. Historical operations (UAE-linked networks targeting Qatar, Saudi-linked operations targeting Iran) have not been updated by Tier 1 to 3 sources since 2024.
actors.doctrine_note: Gulf state operations historically focus on regional adversaries rather than Western democratic institutions. No new Gulf-specific FIMI disclosures were published this week.
actors.headline: No new Gulf state FIMI disclosures this week; no active campaigns documented in current collection window
actors.asymmetric: Gulf state operations are underweighted in institutional FIMI monitoring. EEAS FIMI Explorer does not track Gulf actors. Meta and Google TAG have disclosed Gulf-linked operations in prior years but none in the current cycle.

actors.posture: No documented cross-border FIMI operations in the current collection window. Historical operations (US military psychological operations targeting Middle East audiences) have not been updated by Tier 1 to 3 sources since 2022.
actors.doctrine_note: US operations are absent from EEAS FIMI Explorer by documented political design, not evidentiary threshold. EP PEGA Committee (2022 to 2023) documented specific operations meeting DISARM TTP criteria that were not entered into the FIMI Explorer.
actors.headline: No new US FIMI disclosures this week; EEAS institutional perimeter excludes US operations by political design
actors.asymmetric: US operations run at analytically comparable scale to Russian and Chinese operations but are absent from the EEAS FIMI Explorer by documented political choice. Reading protocol: EEAS FIMI Explorer does not record this actor does not equal EEAS assessed this actor operations as below threshold. It means: EEAS has documented that this actor does not enter its institutional perimeter by political design.

actors.posture: No documented cross-border FIMI operations in the current collection window. Historical operations (Israeli government-linked influence campaigns targeting US and European audiences on Israel-Palestine narratives) have not been updated by Tier 1 to 3 sources since 2023.
actors.doctrine_note: Israeli operations are absent from EEAS FIMI Explorer by documented political design, not evidentiary threshold. EP PEGA Committee (2022 to 2023) documented specific operations meeting DISARM TTP criteria that were not entered into the FIMI Explorer.
actors.headline: No new Israeli FIMI disclosures this week; EEAS institutional perimeter excludes Israeli operations by political design
actors.asymmetric: Israeli operations run at analytically comparable scale to Iranian operations but are absent from the EEAS FIMI Explorer by documented political choice. Reading protocol: EEAS FIMI Explorer does not record this actor does not equal EEAS assessed this actor operations as below threshold. It means: EEAS has documented that this actor does not enter its institutional perimeter by political design.

disclosure_gaps.gap_description: X/Twitter maintains no CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism. The platform has not published a transparency report covering coordinated inauthentic behaviour since Elon Musk acquisition in October 2022. This represents a structural gap in FIMI monitoring — X/Twitter is a primary distribution platform for Doppelganger and other Russian operations, yet no enforcement actions are publicly disclosed.

Meta shifted to semiannual Adversarial Threat Report cadence in 2026. Last report published 11 March 2026 (H1 2026). Next report expected September 2026. The shift from quarterly to semiannual reporting doubles the disclosure lag and reduces timely detection of new operations.

Google TAG last published a bulletin on 29 January 2026 (Q4 2025). A Q1 2026 bulletin is overdue based on historical quarterly cadence. The absence of a Q1 2026 bulletin creates a 15-week disclosure gap for YouTube enforcement actions.

attribution_log.change_reason: Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report provided updated attribution for Endless Mayfly, linking it to Iran International Union of Virtual Media (IUVM). This represents a rare instance of Meta naming a specific Iranian state-adjacent entity rather than using generic Iran-linked language.
attribution_log.headline: Meta updated IUVM attribution for Endless Mayfly in H1 2026 report
attribution_log.asymmetric: The IUVM attribution is methodologically significant because it names a specific entity rather than a generic state-linked descriptor. This level of specificity is uncommon in Meta CIB reporting and signals high internal confidence. However, MF2 applies — the attribution rests on Meta single-source disclosure without independent corroboration from Google TAG, MSTIC, or EEAS.
attribution_log.note: Attribution confidence remains High rather than Confirmed because no independent Tier 1 or Tier 2 source has corroborated the IUVM link. Meta internal attribution methodology is robust but single-platform.

items.headline: Russian operations prioritise narrative persistence over operational security
items.detail: Doppelganger and Portal Kombat demonstrate a consistent Russian doctrine of narrative persistence — accepting repeated platform takedowns and public exposure as an operational cost in exchange for sustained narrative presence. Both operations have been publicly attributed and disrupted multiple times since 2022, yet both continue via platform migration (Telegram, TikTok) and domain regeneration. This signals a strategic calculation that narrative continuity outweighs stealth.
items.summary: The persistence doctrine has implications for platform enforcement strategy. Traditional CIB takedowns are insufficient against actors who prioritise continuity over stealth. Effective countermeasures require sustained infrastructure disruption (domain blocking, hosting provider coordination) rather than one-time account removals.
items.asymmetric: The persistence doctrine is underweighted in mainstream FIMI analysis, which tends to treat takedowns as operational defeats. For Russian actors, takedowns are operational costs, not defeats. The doctrine signals confidence in regeneration capacity and low perceived risk of escalatory consequences.

items.headline: Iranian operations expand into English-language Western targeting
items.detail: Meta H1 2026 report documents a doctrinal shift in Iranian influence operations — expansion from historical Persian-language diaspora targeting into English-language Western audiences. A separate Iranian operation began targeting English-speaking audiences in summer 2025 and was disrupted by Meta in Q4 2025 before it could build an authentic audience. The IUVM/Endless Mayfly attribution confirms this shift is institutionalised rather than experimental.
items.summary: The English-language expansion marks a strategic reorientation for Iranian influence operations. Historical Iranian operations focused on Persian-language diaspora communities and regional adversaries. The shift into English-language Western targeting signals an intent to shape Western public opinion on Israel-Palestine narratives and US domestic politics, not merely to influence diaspora communities.
items.asymmetric: The doctrinal shift is underweighted in FIMI analysis because it occurred gradually across 2024 to 2025 rather than as a single discrete event. The Meta H1 2026 report is the first authoritative confirmation that the shift is institutionalised. This has implications for resource allocation — Iranian operations now require English-language content moderation and Western audience targeting analysis, not merely Persian-language monitoring.

vectors.name: Platform Disclosure Gap
vectors.headline: Meta, Google TAG, and X/Twitter disclosure gaps now exceed 9 to 15 weeks
vectors.summary: Google TAG last published a bulletin on 29 January 2026 (15 weeks ago). Meta last published an Adversarial Threat Report on 11 March 2026 (9 weeks ago). X/Twitter has not published a CIB-equivalent disclosure since October 2022 (187 weeks ago). These gaps create a structural blind spot in FIMI monitoring.
vectors.detail: The disclosure gap is not evenly distributed across actors. Russian and Chinese operations benefit most from the silence — both have documented high-volume infrastructure (Doppelganger, Spamouflage) that persists across platform takedowns. Iranian operations (IUVM) received updated attribution in March but no subsequent enforcement visibility. US and Israeli operations remain outside EEAS institutional perimeter by political design, compounding the asymmetry. The gap disproportionately affects attribution confidence for ongoing campaigns and prevents timely detection of new operations.
vectors.asymmetric: The disclosure gap is a governance failure, not a technical limitation. Meta and Google TAG have the capacity to publish quarterly reports but have chosen to reduce cadence. X/Twitter has eliminated its transparency reporting function entirely. The gap benefits threat actors by reducing the speed and quality of public attribution.

vectors.name: Narrative Persistence
vectors.headline: Doppelganger, Endless Mayfly, and Portal Kombat maintain narrative continuity across 9 to 15 weeks
vectors.summary: All three tracked campaigns demonstrate narrative persistence — coordinated messaging sustained across multiple weeks despite platform enforcement actions. Doppelganger (15 weeks), Portal Kombat (15 weeks), and Endless Mayfly (9 weeks) all maintain active infrastructure and narrative continuity.
vectors.detail: Narrative persistence is a key indicator of operational maturity and resource commitment. Campaigns that persist across multiple enforcement cycles signal actors with regeneration capacity, platform migration capability, and institutional backing. The persistence of Doppelganger and Portal Kombat despite repeated Google TAG and Meta takedowns demonstrates Russian operational doctrine prioritising continuity over stealth.
vectors.asymmetric: Narrative persistence is underweighted in mainstream FIMI analysis, which tends to focus on individual takedown events rather than longitudinal campaign tracking. The persistence metric reveals which actors have the capacity to sustain operations across enforcement cycles — a more meaningful indicator of threat than single-event takedown counts.

vectors.name: Attribution Gap
vectors.headline: US and Israeli operations absent from EEAS FIMI Explorer by political design
vectors.summary: US and Israeli operations run at analytically comparable scale to Russian and Chinese operations but are absent from the EEAS FIMI Explorer by documented political choice. EP PEGA Committee (2022 to 2023) documented specific operations meeting DISARM TTP criteria that were not entered into the FIMI Explorer.
vectors.detail: The attribution gap is not an evidentiary limitation but a governance choice. EEAS institutional perimeter excludes US and Israeli operations by political design, not because operations do not meet FIMI definitional criteria. This creates a structural asymmetry in FIMI monitoring — Russian, Chinese, and Iranian operations are tracked with institutional resources, while US and Israeli operations rely on ad hoc civil society and academic research.
vectors.asymmetric: The attribution gap is a first-order governance failure. It undermines the legitimacy of FIMI monitoring as a field by applying different evidentiary standards to different actors based on political alignment rather than operational behaviour. The gap also creates a methodological blind spot — analysts cannot assess cross-actor TTP convergence or capability parity when three of six primary actors are excluded from institutional monitoring.

vectors.name: AI-enabled FIMI
vectors.headline: Russian and Iranian operations incorporate AI-assisted content production
vectors.summary: Russian operations (Doppelganger, IRA-linked networks) and Iranian operations (IUVM) both incorporate AI-assisted content production. Meta H1 2026 report notes AI-assisted content in Iranian English-language operations. Stanford IO documents AI-generated text in Doppelganger network posts.
vectors.detail: AI-enabled FIMI is not yet a dominant operational mode but is increasingly normalised across Russian and Iranian operations. The use of AI-assisted content production lowers operational costs and increases content volume, but does not yet appear to improve content quality or audience engagement. Current AI-enabled FIMI is characterised by volume amplification rather than precision targeting.
vectors.asymmetric: AI-enabled FIMI is overweighted in mainstream analysis relative to its current operational impact. The presence of AI-generated content is not attribution evidence (MF2 applies). AI tools are widely available and used by both state and non-state actors. The analytical focus should be on operational impact (reach, engagement, narrative persistence) rather than tool presence.

vectors.name: Regulatory Enforcement Gap
vectors.headline: DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions in force but no enforcement actions disclosed this cycle
vectors.summary: EU Digital Services Act Article 40 FIMI provisions entered force in February 2024. No DSA enforcement actions specific to FIMI have been disclosed in the current collection window. The regulatory framework is in place but enforcement visibility is absent.
vectors.detail: The DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions require VLOPs (Very Large Online Platforms) to assess and mitigate risks of foreign information manipulation. The provisions are legally binding but enforcement is opaque. No public DSA enforcement actions have been disclosed for FIMI-specific violations. This creates a regulatory enforcement gap — the legal framework exists but its operational impact is unclear.
vectors.asymmetric: The regulatory enforcement gap is underweighted in FIMI governance analysis. The DSA is frequently cited as a model regulatory framework, but the absence of public enforcement actions means its deterrent effect is unproven. The gap also creates an accountability problem — platforms can claim DSA compliance without demonstrating operational changes.

vectors.name: Cross-Platform Coordination
vectors.headline: Doppelganger demonstrates cross-platform migration following Meta takedowns
vectors.summary: Stanford IO documented that Doppelganger accounts migrated to X/Twitter, Telegram, and TikTok following Meta enforcement actions. This demonstrates cross-platform coordination capability — the operation is not platform-dependent but platform-agnostic.
vectors.detail: Cross-platform coordination is a key indicator of operational maturity. Campaigns that can migrate across platforms following enforcement actions signal actors with multi-platform infrastructure, coordination capacity, and institutional backing. The Doppelganger migration pattern demonstrates Russian operational doctrine prioritising platform diversity over platform-specific optimisation.
vectors.asymmetric: Cross-platform coordination is underweighted in platform enforcement analysis, which tends to focus on single-platform takedowns. The migration pattern reveals that platform-level enforcement is insufficient against actors with cross-platform infrastructure. Effective countermeasures require coordinated cross-platform enforcement and infrastructure disruption.

items.headline: Doppelganger network nodes persist on X/Twitter despite Meta and Google TAG takedowns
items.detail: Stanford Internet Observatory confirmed that accounts linked to Doppelganger domains remained active on X/Twitter after Meta takedowns, posing as Western news outlets and posting in English and French. This demonstrates that X/Twitter lack of CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism creates a safe harbour for operations disrupted on other platforms.
items.asymmetric: The X/Twitter persistence is not incidental but structural. The platform has no CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism and no public enforcement actions for coordinated inauthentic behaviour since October 2022. This creates a predictable migration path for operations disrupted on Meta and Google platforms.

items.headline: Meta H1 2026 report names IUVM as operator of Endless Mayfly — rare entity-level attribution
items.detail: Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report provided updated attribution for Endless Mayfly, linking it to Iran International Union of Virtual Media (IUVM). This represents a rare instance of Meta naming a specific Iranian state-adjacent entity rather than using generic Iran-linked language. The specificity signals high internal confidence.
items.asymmetric: The IUVM attribution is methodologically significant because it names a specific entity rather than a generic state-linked descriptor. This level of specificity is uncommon in Meta CIB reporting. However, the attribution rests on Meta single-source disclosure without independent corroboration from Google TAG, MSTIC, or EEAS.

items.headline: Google TAG Q1 2026 bulletin overdue by 15 weeks based on historical quarterly cadence
items.detail: Google TAG last published a bulletin on 29 January 2026 (Q4 2025). A Q1 2026 bulletin is overdue based on historical quarterly cadence. The absence of a Q1 2026 bulletin creates a 15-week disclosure gap for YouTube enforcement actions. No explanation for the delay has been published.
items.asymmetric: The TAG delay is not an isolated incident but part of a broader pattern of reduced platform transparency. Meta shifted to semiannual reporting in 2026. X/Twitter eliminated transparency reporting entirely in 2022. The cumulative effect is a structural degradation of FIMI monitoring capacity.

module_9.title: Regulatory and Policy

no_change.item: EU Digital Services Act Article 40 FIMI provisions
no_change.status: In force since February 2024. No new enforcement actions disclosed this cycle.
no_change.next_trigger: First public DSA enforcement action for FIMI-specific violation would constitute material change.

no_change.status: Active. No new annual report or methodology updates published this cycle.
no_change.next_trigger: Publication of EEAS FIMI annual report 2025 (expected Q2 2026) would constitute material change.

module_9.friction_analysis: Regulatory friction is highest at the enforcement layer. The DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions are legally binding but no public enforcement actions have been disclosed. This creates an accountability gap — platforms can claim DSA compliance without demonstrating operational changes. The absence of enforcement visibility undermines the deterrent effect of the regulatory framework.
module_9.dsa_fimi_status: DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions entered force in February 2024. The provisions require VLOPs to assess and mitigate risks of foreign information manipulation. Obligations are live but enforcement is opaque. No public DSA enforcement actions have been disclosed for FIMI-specific violations. The critical path runs through enforcement visibility — without public disclosure of enforcement actions, the operational impact of the DSA FIMI provisions remains unproven.

civil_society_research.item: Stanford Internet Observatory analysis of Doppelganger persistence on X/Twitter
civil_society_research.summary: Stanford IO confirmed that accounts linked to Doppelganger domains remained active on X/Twitter after Meta takedowns, posing as Western news outlets and posting in English and French. This demonstrates that X/Twitter lack of CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism creates a safe harbour for operations disrupted on other platforms.

governance_gaps.analysis: Meta shifted to semiannual reporting in 2026. Google TAG Q1 2026 bulletin is overdue by 15 weeks. X/Twitter has not published a CIB-equivalent disclosure since October 2022. The cumulative effect is a structural degradation of FIMI monitoring capacity.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: The disclosure cadence degradation is not a technical limitation but a governance choice. Platforms have the capacity to publish quarterly reports but have chosen to reduce cadence or eliminate transparency reporting entirely. The degradation benefits threat actors by reducing the speed and quality of public attribution.

governance_gaps.gap: EEAS institutional perimeter excludes US and Israeli operations by political design
governance_gaps.analysis: US and Israeli operations run at analytically comparable scale to Russian and Chinese operations but are absent from the EEAS FIMI Explorer by documented political choice. EP PEGA Committee (2022 to 2023) documented specific operations meeting DISARM TTP criteria that were not entered into the FIMI Explorer.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: The institutional perimeter gap is a first-order governance failure. It undermines the legitimacy of FIMI monitoring as a field by applying different evidentiary standards to different actors based on political alignment rather than operational behaviour. The gap also creates a methodological blind spot — analysts cannot assess cross-actor TTP convergence or capability parity when three of six primary actors are excluded from institutional monitoring.

items.summary: Russian operations (Doppelganger, IRA-linked networks) and Iranian operations (IUVM) both incorporate AI-assisted content production. Meta H1 2026 report notes AI-assisted content in Iranian English-language operations. Stanford IO documents AI-generated text in Doppelganger network posts. Current AI-enabled FIMI is characterised by volume amplification rather than precision targeting.
items.asymmetric: AI-enabled FIMI is overweighted in mainstream analysis relative to its current operational impact. The presence of AI-generated content is not attribution evidence (MF2 applies). AI tools are widely available and used by both state and non-state actors. The analytical focus should be on operational impact (reach, engagement, narrative persistence) rather than tool presence.

asymmetric_flags: AI-enabled FIMI is overweighted in mainstream analysis relative to its current operational impact. The presence of AI-generated content is not attribution evidence.

module_13.title: Information Environment

structural_trends.title: Platform disclosure cadence degradation
structural_trends.trend: Meta shifted to semiannual reporting in 2026. Google TAG Q1 2026 bulletin is overdue by 15 weeks. X/Twitter has not published a CIB-equivalent disclosure since October 2022. The cumulative effect is a structural degradation of FIMI monitoring capacity.

asymmetric_flags: The disclosure cadence degradation is not a technical limitation but a governance choice. Platforms have the capacity to publish quarterly reports but have chosen to reduce cadence or eliminate transparency reporting entirely.

module_14.title: Amplification and Power Structures

domains.note: Russian, Chinese, and Iranian operations maintain documented high-volume infrastructure. No new amplification network disclosures this cycle.
Russian operations demonstrate cross-platform migration capability following enforcement actions. Doppelganger persists on X/Twitter despite Meta and Google TAG takedowns.
Attribution capacity remains concentrated on Russian, Chinese, and Iranian operations. US and Israeli operations excluded from EEAS institutional perimeter by political design.

asymmetric_flags: Platform access concentration is increasing for Russian operations due to X/Twitter lack of CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism.

Obligations are live but enforcement is opaque. No public DSA enforcement actions have been disclosed for FIMI-specific violations.

eeas_ras_fimi.summary: EEAS Rapid Alert System and FIMI Toolbox remain active. No new annual report or methodology updates published this cycle. EEAS FIMI annual report 2025 expected Q2 2026.

irgc_eu_terrorist_designation.summary: IRGC EU terrorist designation remains under consideration. No material change this cycle.

nato_stratcom_coe.summary: NATO StratCom Centre of Excellence continues FIMI research and analysis. No new publications this cycle.

x_twitter.reading_protocol: X/Twitter maintains no CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism. Platform has not published transparency report covering coordinated inauthentic behaviour since Elon Musk acquisition in October 2022. This represents a structural gap in FIMI monitoring.

meta.reading_protocol: Meta shifted to semiannual Adversarial Threat Report cadence in 2026. Last report published 11 March 2026 (H1 2026). Next report expected September 2026. The shift from quarterly to semiannual reporting doubles the disclosure lag.

google_tag.reading_protocol: Google TAG last published bulletin 29 January 2026 (Q4 2025). A Q1 2026 bulletin is overdue based on historical quarterly cadence. The absence of a Q1 2026 bulletin creates a 15-week disclosure gap.

mstic.reading_protocol: Microsoft MSTIC publishes ad hoc influence operation disclosures. No new MSTIC disclosures this cycle.

tiktok.reading_protocol: TikTok publishes limited CIB-equivalent reporting. No new TikTok FIMI disclosures this cycle.

value.summary: EEAS Rapid Alert System and FIMI Toolbox are active. The 4th FIMI Threat Report was published in March 2026, documenting Russian operational dominance at 29 percent of 2025 incidents and identifying Armenia as the next electoral FIMI target using the Moldova playbook.

proposed_patches.material_change_justification: Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report published March 2026 documenting Iranian operation disruption

value.dsa_action: Proactive DSA compliance with regular CIB disclosures
value.reading_protocol: Meta provides the most consistent platform-level FIMI disclosure among major platforms. Six-month delay between disruption and public disclosure limits real-time monitoring utility.
